front: Play: in this play, the Actor hangs himself after the idealist Luka disappears, prompting Satine to mutter 'He spoiled the song!'
Play: in this play, the thief Vaska Pepel kills his landlord Kostilyoff, encouraged by Kostilyoff's wife Vassilisa
Play: in this play, an idealistic old wanderer named Luka comforts residents of a boarding house and tells the Actor about a sanatorium that could cure his alcoholism
Play: in this play, Luka tells an anecdote about a man who hanged himself because there was no 'land of righteousness'
Play: in this play, the cardsharp Satine declares 'Man! It has a proud ring to it!' in a monologue on human dignity
Play: in this play, the Actor claims he cannot sweep the floor because his 'organism is poisoned with alcohol'
Play: in this play, Natasha's sister Vassilisa burns her legs by throwing boiling oil on them
Play: both Jean Renoir and Akira Kurosawa adapted this play about residents of a run-down lodging house
Play: Satine defends Luka in this play, saying 'He lied out of sheer pity for you... He understood man—you don't!'
Short Story: in this story, a group of 26 pretzel bakers idolize the young Tanya but turn on her after she is seduced by a soldier
Novel: in this novel, Pelagea (Nilovna) becomes radicalized through her son Pavel's revolutionary activities; Pavel is arrested at a May Day demonstration
Novel: this 1906 novel is considered the first major work of socialist realism, and was adapted into a 1926 film by montage theorist Vsevolod Pudovkin
Play: in this play, the chemist Protasov is accused of spreading cholera and a mob attacks him, while his sister Lisa foresees the danger
Author: this author's poem about a 'proud, black demon of the tempest' flying unafraid in a storm got him arrested and earned him the epithet 'Stormy Petrel'
Author: this author wrote an essay on the Lumière brothers' cinematograph beginning 'Last night I was in the kingdom of the shadows,' published under the pseudonym 'I. M. Pacatus'
Author: this author's autobiography My Childhood recalls being beaten unconscious by his grandfather, who owned a dye shop
Author: this author was exiled to Capri, where he wrote Tales of Italy, and was later lured back to Russia by fake fan mail; his birth city Nizhny Novgorod was renamed after him
Author: Lenin wrote a letter to this author arguing that 'god-building' and 'god-seeking' differ 'no more than a yellow devil differs from a blue devil'
Author: this author wrote 'Untimely Thoughts' in 1918, describing Lenin as tyrannical, which contributed to his second exile
Author: Stalin personally served as one of this Russian author's pallbearers
Author: in Reminiscences of Leo Nikolaevich Tolstoy, this author imagined Tolstoy 'lying in his coffin like a smooth stone at the bottom of a brook'
Author: this author's debut play The Philistines, subtitled 'Scenes in the House of Bessemenov,' won the Griboyedov prize
